title: - MVP-BERT: REDESIGNING VOCABULARIES FOR CHI - NESE BERT AND MULTI VOCAB PRETRAINING [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - MVP-BERT: REDESIGNING VOCABULARIES FOR CHI - NESE BERT AND MULTI VOCAB PRETRAINING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Despite the development of pre-trained language models (PLMs) significantly raise the performances of various Chinese natural language processing (NLP) tasks, the vocabulary for these Chinese PLMs remain to be the one provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018), which is based on Chinese characters. Second, the masked language model pre-training is based on a single vocabulary, which limits its downstream task performances. In this work, we first propose a novel method, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , to form the vocabulary of Chinese BERT, with the help of Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization. Then we propose three versions of multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) to improve the models expressiveness. Experiments show that: (a) compared with char based vocabulary, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ does not only improves the performances of Chinese PLMs on sentence level tasks, it can also improve efficiency; (b) MVP improves PLMs’ downstream performance, especially it can improve _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ’s performances on sequence labeling tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The pretrained language models (PLMs) including BERT Devlin et al. (2018) and its variants Yang et al. (2019); Liu et al. (2019b) have been proven beneficial for many natural language processing (NLP) tasks, such as text classification, question answering Rajpurkar et al. (2018) and natural language inference (NLI) Bowman et al. (2015), on English, Chinese and many other languages. Despite they brings amazing improvements for Chinese NLP tasks, most of the Chinese PLMs still use the vocabulary (vocab) provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018). Google Chinese Bert is a character (char) based model, since it splits the Chinese characters with blank spaces. In the pre-BERT era, a part of the literature on Chinese natural language processing (NLP) first do Chinese word segmentation (CWS) to divide text into sequences of words, and use a word based vocab in NLP models Xu et al. (2015); Zou et al. (2013). There are a lot of arguments on which vocab a Chinese NLP model should adopt.

The advantages of char based models are clear. First, Char based vocab is smaller, thus reducing the model size. Second, it does not rely on CWS, thus avoiding word segmentation error, which can directly result in performance gain in span based tasks such as named entity recognition (NER). Third, char-based models are less vulnerable to data sparsity or the presence of out-of-vocab (OOV) words, and thus less prone to over-fitting (Li et al. (2019)). However, word based model has its advantages. First, it will result in shorter sequences than char based counterparties, thus are faster. Second, words are less ambiguous, thus may be helpful for models to learn the semantic meanings of words. Third, with a word based model, exposure biases may be reduced in text generation tasks (Zhao et al. (2013)). Another branch of literature try to strike a balance between the two by combining word based embedding with character based embedding Yin et al. (2016); Dong et al. (2016).

In this article, we try to strike a balance between the char based model and word based model and provides alternative approaches for building a vocab for Chinese PLMs. In this article, there are three approaches to build a vocab for Chinese PLMs: (1) following Devlin et al. (2018), separate the Chinese characters with white spaces, and then learn a sub-word tokenizer (denote as _char_ ); (2) first segment the sentences with a CWS toolkit like jieba[1] , and then learn a sub-word tokenizer (denoted

> 1https://github.com/fxsjy/jieba

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

as _seg tok_ ); (3) do CWS and kept the high-frequency words as tokens and low-frequency words will be tokenized by _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ (denoted as _seg_ ). See Figure 1 for their workflow of processing an input sentence. Note that the first one is essentially the same with the vocab of Google Chinese BERT.

Inspired by the previous work that incorporate multiple vocabularies (vocabs) or combine multiple vocabs in an natural wayYin et al. (2016); Dong et al. (2016), we also investigate a series of strategies, which we will call Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies. The first version of MVP is to incorporate a hierarchical structure to combine the char based vocab and word based vocab. From the viewpoint of model forward pass, the embeddings of Chinese characters are aggregated to form the vector representations of multi-gram words or tokens, which then are fed into transformer encoders, and then the word based vocab will be used in masked language model (MLM) training. We will denote this version of MVP as MVP _hier_ . Note that in MVP _hier_ , the char based vocab is built by splitting the Chinese words in the word based vocab into Chinese chars, and non-Chinese tokens are kept the same. We will denote this strategy as MVP _hier_ ( _V_ ), where _V_ is a word based vocab.

The second version of MVP (denoted as MVP _pair_ ) is to employ a pair of vocabs in MLM. Due to limited resources, in this article we only consider the pair between _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _char_ . MVP _pair_ is depicted in Figure 2(c). In MVP _pair_ , a sentence (or a concatenation of multiple sentences in pretraining), is processed and tokenized both in _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _char_ , and the two sentences are encoded by two parameter-sharing transformer encoders. Whole word masking Cui et al. (2019b) is applied for pretraining. For example, the word ” `篮球` ” (basketball) is masked. The left encoder, which is with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , has to predict the single masked token is ” `篮球` ”, and the right encoder has to predict ” `篮` ” and ” `球` ” for two masked tokens. MLM loss from both sides will be added with weights. With MVP _pair_ , parameter sharing enables the single vocab model to absorb information from the other vocab, thus enhancing its expressiveness. Note that after pre-training, one can either keep one of the encoder or both encoders for downstream finetuning. We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i_ ), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are two different vocabs, _i_ = _s_ means only the encoder with _V_ 1 is kept for finetuning (single vocab model), and _i_ = _e_ means both encoders are kept (ensemble model).

The third version of MVP (denoted as MVP _obj_ ) is depicted in Figure 2(b). In MVP _obj_ , the sentence is encoded only once with a fine-grained vocab, and MLM task with that vocab is conducted. As in the figure, he word ” `喜欢` ” (like) is masked, and under the vocab of _char_ , the PLM has to predict ” `喜` ” and ” `欢` ” for the two masked tokens. As additional training objective, we will employ a more coarse-grained vocab like _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and ask the model to use the starting token (” `喜` ”)’s representation to predict the original word under _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.

Figure 1: An illustration of how to process input sentence into tokens under different methods we

Extensive experiments and ablation studies are conducted. We select BPE implemented by sentencepiece[2] as the sub-word model, and Albert **?** (base model) as our PLM. Pre-training is done on Chinese Wikipedia corpus[3] , which is also the corpus on which we build the different vocabs. After

> 2https://github.com/google/sentencepiece

> 3https://dumps.wikimedia.org/zhwiki/latest/

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) MVP hier (b) MVP obj
(c) MVP pair
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 2: The architectures for the three versions of MVP strategies.

pretraining, the three vocab building methods and three MVP strategies are compared on a series of Chinese benchmark datasets, 4 of which are sentence classification tasks, 3 are sequence labeling tasks. The experimental results demonstrate the following take-aways: 1) directly use Chinese words as vocab ( _seg_ ) does not perform well; 2) _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ based ALBERT consistently performs better than _char_ and _seg_ on sentence classification tasks, but not sequence labeling tasks; 3) MVP strategies can help to improve a single vocab model on both types of tasks, especially it can help _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ on sequence labeling tasks; 4) MVP _pair_ ensemble are the best model, but it comes with a higher inference time; 5) MVP _obj_ help to provide a better single vocab model than MVP _pair_ .

We now summaries the following contributions in this work.

- experiments on three ways of building new vocab for Chinese BERT, and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , the combination of CWS and subword tokenization is novel.

- We propose 3 MVP strategies for enhancing the Chinese PLMs.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Before and since Devlin et al. (2018), a large amount of literature on pretrained language model appear and push the NLP community forward with a speed that has never been witnessed before. Peters et al. (2018) is one of the earliest PLMs that learns contextualized representations of words. GPTs Radford et al. (2018; 2019) and BERT Devlin et al. (2018) take advantages of Transformer Vaswani et al. (2017). GPTs are uni-directional and make prediction on the input text in an auto-regressive manner, and BERT is bi-directional and make prediction on the whole or part of the input text. In its core, what makes BERT so powerful are the pretraing tasks, i.e., Mask language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP), where the former is more important. Since

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

BERT, a series of improvements have been proposed. The first branch of literature improves the model architecture of BERT. ALBERT Lan et al. (2019) makes BERT more light-weighted by embedding factorization and progressive cross layer parameter sharing. Zaheer et al. (2020) improve BERT’s performance on longer sequences by employing sparser attention.

The second branch of literature improve the training of BERT. Liu et al. (2019b) stabilize and improve the training of BERT with larger corpus. More work have focused on new language pretraining tasks. ALBERT Lan et al. (2019) introduce sentence order prediction (SOP). StructBERT Wang et al. (2019) designs two novel pre-training tasks, word structural task and sentence structural task, for learning of better representations of tokens and sentences. ERNIE 2.0 Sun et al. (2019) proposes a series of pretraining tasks and applies continual learning to incorporate these tasks. ELECTRA Clark et al. (2020) has a GAN-style pretraining task for efciently utilizing all tokens in pretraining. Our work is closely related to this branch of literature by design a series of novel pretraining objective by incorporating multiple vocabularies. Our proposed tasks focus on intra-sentence contextual learning, and it can be easily incorporated with other sentence structural tasks like SOP.

Another branch of literature look into the role of words in pre-training. Although not mentioned in Devlin et al. (2018), the authors propose whole word masking in their open-source repository, which is effective for pretraining BERT. In SpanBERT Joshi et al. (2019), text spans are masked in pretraining and the learned model can substantially enhance the performances of span selection tasks. It is indicated that word segmentation is especially important for Chinese PLMs. Cui et al. (2019a) and Sun et al. (2019) both show that masking tokens in the units of natural Chinese words instead of single Chinese characters can significantly improve Chinese PLMs. In this work, compared to literature, we propose to re-design the vocabulary of the Chinese BERT by combining word segmentation and sub-word tokenizations.

## 3 OUR METHODS

In this section, we present our methods for rebuilding the vocab for Chinese PLMs, and introduce our series of MVP strategies.

## 3.1 BUILDING THE VOCABS

We investigate four work-flows to process the text inputs, each corresponding to a different vocab (or a group of vocabs) (Figure 1). We first introduce the single vocab models, _char_ , _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _seg_ . For char based vocab _char_ , Chinese characters in the corpus are treated as words in English and are separated with blank spaces and a sub-word tokenizer is learned. _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ requires the sentences in corpus to be segmented and a sub-word tokenizer like BPE are learned on the segmented sentences. Note that in _seg tok_ , some natural word will be splitted into pieces, but there are still many tokens that have multiple Chinese chars. Finally, _seg_ with size _N_ is built with the following procedures: a) do CWS on the corpus; b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies; d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab. Note that some of the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ will be dropped.

## 3.2 MULTI-VOCAB PRETRAINING (MVP)

In this subsection, we will introduce MVP, which is a derivation of MLM task by Devlin et al. (2018). MVP has three versions, MVP _hier_ , MVP _obj_ and MVP _pair_ , all of which have one thing in common, that is, they require more than one vocab to implement pre-training.

Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of MVP _hier_ and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences. Two vocab, a fine-grained vocab _Vf_ , and a more coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ , are combined in a hierarchical way. Sequences are first tokenized via _Vc_ , and then the Chinese tokens (if containing multiple Chinese characters) are splitted into single characters. Chinese characters and non-Chinese tokens are embedded into vectors. Then representations of chars inside a word is aggregated into the representation of this word, which is further fed into the transformer encoder. During MLM task, whole word masking is applied, that is, we will mask 15% of the tokens

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

in the _Vc_ . For example in Figure 2(a), ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” (like) is masked, thus in the char sequence, two tokens ”” _and_ ””” Let **x** and **y** denote the sequences of tokens with length _lx_ and _ly_ , for the same sentence under _Vc_ and _Vf_ , in which a part of tokens are masked. Denote **x** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vc_ . The loss function for MVP _hier_ is
in which I _[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] _[ i]_[-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]

In MVP _obj_ , a sentence is tokenized and embedded in a fine-grained _Vf_ (e.g., a char based vocab), and MLM task on _Vf_ is conducted. However, different from the vanilla MLM, another MLM task based on a more coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ is added. For example, encoded representations of the chars ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” inside the word ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is aggregated to the vector representation of the word, and an auxiliary MLM layer is tasked to predict the word based on _Vc_ . For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.[4] Denote **x** _[mask]_ and **y** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vf_ and _Vc_ respectively. The loss function for MVP _obj_ is as follows:

in which I _[x] i_[and][I] _[y] i_[are][variables][with binary][values indicating whether][the] _[i]_[-th token][is masked][in] sequence x and y respectively. Here _λ_ is the coefficient which measures the relative importance of the auxiliary MLM task.

Now we introduce MVP _pair_ , which is the most resource-demanding version of MVP, but it will be proven beneficial. Our goal is to enhance the model with a single vocab, by introducing additional encoder with another vocab and a corresponding MLM task. In this strategy, a sentence is tokenized and embedded with two vocabs, _V_ 1 and _V_ 2, which will be fed into separate transformer encoders. Transformer encoders will share the same parameters, but the embedding layers are separate. For example in Figure 2(c), ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is masked, and thus two tokens on the left sequence and(or) one token on the right sequence are masked. The left encoder and MLM layer is tasked to recover the two tokens ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ”. And on the right, the other MLM layer needs to recover ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ”. Through parameter sharing, self-supervised signals from one vocab is transferred to the model with the other vocab. Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP _pair_ is

If after MVP _pair_ pretraining we decide to only keep one of the encoder, we will call this model single vocab MVP _pair_ . Otherwise, we can call the model as ensemble MVP _pair_ . For single mode MVP _pair_ , finetuning is the same with vanilla ALBERT. Finetuning for ensemble mode MVP _pair_ is different. For sentence classification, the pooled vectors on both [CLS] tokens will be concatenated to be the feature vector of the classifier. When doing sequence labeling tasks, two ways of ensemble can be conducted, which we will use the example from Figure 2(c) to illustrate. The first approach is to concatenate the features from fine-grained encoder to the coarse-grained encoder. That is, representations of ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” are aggregated to the representation of ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ”, and it will be concatenated to the representation from the coarse-grained encoder on the right. We will call this approach fine-to-coarse ensemble. The other approach is coarse-to-fine ensemble, which is to concatenate the representation of ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” from the coarse-grained encoder to ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” from the fine-grained encoder. Then the labels of ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” are predicted.

For notational convenience, we will denote the model pretrained with MVP _hier_ strategy and vocab _V_ as MVP _hier_ ( _V_ ). MVP _obj_ with a fine-grained vocab _Vf_ and a coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ are denoted
> 4Due to limited resources available, we leave to future work to investigate whether alternative aggregators can bring improvements.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

as MVP _obj_ ( _Vf , Vc_ ). MVP _pair_ with two vocab _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 is denoted as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i, j_ ), where _i_ can be _single_ , meaning only keep the encoder from _V_ 1 for finetuning, or _ensemble_ , meaning keep both encoders, and _j_ can be _ftc_ (short for fine-to-coarse) or _ctf_ (coarse-to-fine), which is two approaches for finetuning MVP _pair_ on sequence labeling tasks. Note that if _i_ equals _single_ , we will neglect the parameter _j_ .

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 SETUP

For pre-training corpus, we use Chinese Wikipedia. The vocab size is 21128 for _char_ and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . 50.38% of _seg_ are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
In this article, we adopt jieba as our CWS tool.[5] To keep 70% of the vocab as natural words from CWS, and cover 99.95% of the corpus, _seg_ is built with vocab size 69341.

For MVP _hier_ , we consider MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ). For MVP _obj_ , we consider MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _obj_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ), with _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0. For MVP _pair_ , we consider MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), the combination of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and char based vocab, with _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0. Note that to maintain the consistency for non-Chinese tokens, the char based vocab in MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) is derived from _seg tok_ by splitting Chinese characters of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ tokens into characters, not exactly the same with _char_ .

For pretraining, whole word masking is adopted, and total 15% of the words (from CWS) in the corpus are masked, which are then tokenized into different tokens under different vocabs. For MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), 1/3 of the time only tokens _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocabs are predicted.

In this article, all models use the ALBERT as encoder. We make use of a smaller parameter settings, that is, the number of layer is 3, the embedding size is 128 and the hidden size is 256. Other ALBERT configurations remain the same with ALBERT Lan et al. (2019). The pretraining hyper-parameters are almost the same with ALBERT Lan et al. (2019). The maximum sequence length is 512. Here, the sequence length is counted under the coarse-grained vocab for MVP _hier_ , fine-grained vocab for MVP _obj_ , and the longer one of the two sequences under the two vocabs for MVP _pair_ . The batch size is 1024, and all the model are trained for 12.5k steps. The pretraining optimizer is LAMB and the learning rate is 1e-4. For finetuning, the sequence length is 256, the learning rate is 2e-5, the optimizer is Adam Kingma & Ba (2015) and the batch size is set as the power of 2 and so that each epoch contains less than 1000 steps. Each model is run on a given task for 10 times and the average performance scores and standard deviations are reported for reproducibility.

## 4.2 BENCHMARK TASKS

For downstream tasks, we select 4 text classification tasks: (1) ChnSentiCorp (chn)[6] , a hotel review dataset; (2) Book review[7] (book ~~r~~ eview), collected from Douban[8] by Liu et al. (2019a). For sentence pair classification tasks, we include the following 4 datasets: (1) XNLI (xnli) from Conneau et al. (2018) ; (2) LCQMC (lcqmc) Liu et al. (2018); (3) NLPCC-DBQA[9] (nlpcc ~~d~~ bqa), a question-answer matching task in the open domain; (4) Law QA Liu et al. (2019a), a QA matching task in the legal domain. We also investigate three NER tasks. MSRA NER (msra) Levow (2006) is from open

Table 1: Main results on the Chinese benchmark datasets. For each task and each model, experiments are repeated for 10 times, and the average and standard deviation of the scores are reported.
domain, Finance NER[10] (fin) is from financial domain, and CCKS NER[11] (ccks) is collected from medical records.

## 4.3 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

We first compare the three single vocab models. We find that word based vocabs _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _seg_ perform better than _char_ on all sentence classification tasks, and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ outperforms _seg_ even though having less parameters. However, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is worse than or comparable to _char_ on three NER tasks, where these performance gaps are only partially explained by the CWS errors[12] . We believe that its disadvantages on sequence labeling tasks is due to sparcity of tokens, i.e., each token has less training samples, thus are less well trained for token level classification. From the above results, we show that both CWS and subword tokenization are important to improve the expressiveness of Chinese PLMs in sentence level tasks via vocab re-designing. In addition, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is more efficient than _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . we can observe a 1.35x speed up when changing the vocab from _char_ to _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ .

The lower rows of Table 1 also demonstrates the effectiveness of MVP training. Note that in this table, we report MVP _obj_ with _λ_ = 2 _._ 0, and MVP _pair_ with _λ_ = 1 _._ 0. First, we can see MVPs consistently improve the models’ performances, among which the largest improvements come from MVP _obj_ and ensemble MVP _pair_ . Second, for _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , keeping only one encoder provides improvements on sequence labeling tasks, but the improvements are less than that provided by MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ) on sentence level tasks. Third, note that for NER tasks, ensemble MVP _pair_ performs better than single MVP _pair_ , and coarse-to-fine ensemble is more effective than fine-to-coarse ensemble. Fourth, MVP _obj_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ) achieve performances that are close to MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, char, e_ ) on lcqmc, with less than half of the time in pre-training and fine-tuning. But the model’s improvements on NER tasks are less significant. Notice that for pretraining and finetuning, MVP _pair_ takes almost twice the resources and time than MVP _obj_ . Thus, for resources limited scenarios, MVP _obj_ could be a more suitable choice.

## 4.4 EFFECTS OF _λ_

In this section, we investigate how the relative importance coefficient _λ_ for MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) affects the ALBERT models’ downstream performances. Here for MVP _pair_ , _λ_ is assigned to the loss term from coarse-grained vocab. We set _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run per-

> 10https://embedding.github.io/evaluation/extrinsic

> 11https://biendata.com/competition/CCKS2017 2/

> 12CWS errors accounts for only less than 0.3% of the errors on CCKS.

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Figure 3: How the value of coefficient _λ_ affects the models’ downstream performances.

training under each of the setting. Finetuning results on _lcqmc_ and _msra_ are reported in Figure 3. For ensemble MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, e_ ), _λ_ = 1 _._ 0 provides the best performances. And for MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, s_ ), increasing _λ_ worsen the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks, and vice versa for MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, char, s_ ).

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we first propose a novel method, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , to re-build the vocabulary of Chinese BERT, with the help of Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization. Then we propose three versions of multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) to improve the models’ performance. Experiments show that: (a) compared with char based vocabulary, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ does not only improves the performances of Chinese PLMs on sentence level tasks, it can also improve efficiency; (b) MVP improves PLMs’ downstream performance, especially it can improve _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ’s performances on sequence labeling tasks.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper explores redesigning vocabularies for Chinese BERT models by moving beyond character-based approaches toward Word Segmentation (CWS) combined with subword tokenization, alongside a Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) framework. While the conceptual motivation is strong and the experimental results on downstream tasks show promise, the paper suffers from serious mathematical notation errors in the loss functions, internal nomenclature inconsistencies (specifically regarding MVP_obj vs MVP_pair), and a significant lack of detail regarding the hierarchical aggregation methods and sequence labeling ensemble architectures, which hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Serious Mathematical Errors in Loss Function Definitions
comments.quote: The loss function for MVP_obj is as follows: \mathcal{L}_{MVP\_obj}=\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}} I_{[x] i} \cdot \log P(x_{i} \mid \mathbf{x}_{\setminus mask})+\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}} I_{[y] i} \cdot \log P(y_{i} \mid \mathbf{y}_{\setminus mask})
comments.explanation: In standard Masked Language Modeling (MLM), we maximize the log-likelihood, meaning the loss function (to be minimized) should be the negative log-likelihood. The equations provided (for MVP_hier, MVP_obj, and MVP_pair) are missing the negative sign. A literal implementation of the provided formulas would lead to gradient ascent rather than descent. Furthermore, in Eq 2 and 3, the notation $P(y_i | y_{\setminus mask})$ is used, but in MVP_obj, $y$ (the coarse-grained word) is predicted from $x$ (the fine-grained characters), so the conditional probability should likely reflect that cross-vocab dependency.

comments.title: Nomenclature Inconsistency and Figure-Text Mismatch
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP_pair(V_1, V_2), where V_1 and V_2 are a pair of vocabs and V_1 is the more fine-grained one. [later] ...MVP_obj(Vf, Vc).
comments.explanation: In the paragraph describing MVP_obj, the authors explicitly write 'We will denote this strategy as MVP_pair(V_1, V_2)' despite previously defining the strategy as MVP_obj. This is highly confusing because MVP_pair is a distinct strategy described in the next paragraph. Additionally, Figure 2(b) labels the method 'MVP obj', but the descriptive text for that figure suggests a mixture of terms. This makes it difficult to track which strategy is being evaluated in the tables.

comments.title: Inconsistent References to Vocabulary Efficiency
comments.quote: In addition, seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok. we can observe a 1.35x speed up when changing the vocab from char to seg_tok.
comments.explanation: The text contains a clear typo ('seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok'). While a reader might infer from context that they meant 'char', the comparison of efficiency is underspecified. Shorter sequence lengths usually lead to speedups in Transformers, but word-based vocabularies often have larger embedding matrices. The authors do not clarify if the '1.35x speed up' refers to training throughput (steps/sec) or convergence time, or if it counts the overhead of CWS preprocessing.

comments.title: Ambiguous Method for Aggregation and Sequence Labeling Ensemble
comments.quote: For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.
comments.explanation: The 'BERT-style pooler' traditionally refers to the [CLS] token used for sentence-level tasks via a dense layer and tanh activation. Using the 'starting token' of a subword sequence to represent a whole word is usually referred to as 'first-subword' pooling. It is unclear if the authors simply took the hidden state or applied the standard BERT pooling weights (dense + tanh). This is critical for reproducibility, especially for the MVP_hier and MVP_obj models where this aggregation is a central feature.

comments.title: Unspecified Parameter λ in Main Results
comments.quote: Note that in this table, we report MVP_obj with λ = 2.0, and MVP_pair with λ = 1.0.
comments.explanation: While these values are chosen for the main results table, the paper later shows in Figure 3 that the performance is quite sensitive to λ (e.g., for MSRA NER, higher λ significantly degrades performance for 'single' models). The justification for why these specific λ values were chosen for Table 1 across all tasks (classification vs NER) is not provided, making it unclear if the results are optimal or cherry-picked.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound investigation into vocabulary design and multi-vocabulary pretraining for Chinese BERT models. The introduction of seg_tok as a hybrid of word segmentation and subword tokenization is a thoughtful compromise between character- and word-based approaches. The three MVP strategies are conceptually clear, with empirical validation across multiple tasks. However, there are several technical inconsistencies in the loss functions and notation that undermine confidence in reproducibility, particularly in the description of MVP_obj and MVP_pair. While the overall trends in results appear robust, the mathematical formulations require clarification to ensure correctness.

comments.title: Incorrect auxiliary loss indexing in MVP_obj loss function
comments.quote:
Denote **x** _[mask]_ and **y** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vf_ and _Vc_ respectively. The loss function for MVP _obj_ is 

in which I _[x] i_[and][I] _[y] i_[are][variables][with binary][values indicating whether][the] _[i]_[-th token][is masked][in] sequence x and y respectively.
comments.explanation: The loss function for MVP_obj uses I_[y]i_ to indicate masking in the coarse-grained vocabulary sequence y, but y is not actually a separate input sequence — it's derived from aggregating fine-grained tokens. More critically, the auxiliary prediction is based on the representation of the starting token of a span (e.g., '喜' for '喜欢'), yet the loss term L_MLM(y) suggests predicting at the token level of Vc. However, the auxiliary task is not token-level MLM but rather a word-level prediction using only the first subtoken's representation. Thus, the masking indicator I_[y]i_ does not correspond to actual inputs in the same way as in standard MLM, and the summation over i for L_MLM(y) is misleading. The loss should reflect that only certain positions (aggregated spans) are used for the auxiliary objective, not a full masking over a separately tokenized y. This creates a mismatch between the described mechanism and the formal loss.

comments.title: Misleading loss formulation for MVP_pair with asymmetric vocab masking
comments.quote: For MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), 1/3 of the time only tokens _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocabs are predicted.
comments.explanation: The text describes a scheduling where only one vocabulary's MLM objective is active at training time with 1/3 probability each for seg_tok, char, and both. However, the presented loss function for MVP_pair sums both losses with a fixed weight λ, implying simultaneous optimization. This creates a contradiction: if only one side is masked and predicted at a time, the loss should be selected stochastically, not summed. The current formulation suggests both objectives are always computed, which conflicts with the stated training procedure. This could mislead a reader attempting to reproduce the method, as the implementation would need to conditionally apply losses rather than combine them additively every step.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for MVP_pair model variants in text vs. formal definition
comments.quote: MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i, j_ ), where _i_ can be _single_ , meaning only keep the encoder from _V_ 1 for finetuning, or _ensemble_ , meaning keep both encoders, and _j_ can be _ftc_ (short for fine-to-coarse) or _ctf_ (coarse-to-fine)... Note that if _i_ equals _single_ , we will neglect the parameter _j_.
comments.explanation: In the text, the parameter i in MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j) is said to be 'single' or 'ensemble', but in Table 1, the models are labeled as MVP_pair(_char, seg_tok, s_) and MVP_pair(_seg_tok, char, e, ftc_), using 's' and 'e' instead. While 's' and 'e' likely stand for 'single' and 'ensemble', this abbreviation is not defined in the paper. Moreover, in one case (MVP_pair(_seg_tok, char, s_)) the j parameter is omitted as expected, but in others it's included. However, the inconsistency is that in the subscript it's written as s_ with an underscore, suggesting formatting corruption. This undermines precise interpretation, especially when comparing results across models. Standard notation should be preserved for clarity.

comments.title: MVP_hier figure reference error and missing loss description
comments.quote: Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of MVP _hier_ and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences.
comments.explanation: The text states that Figure 2(a) depicts both the architecture and procedure of MVP_hier, but then in the next sentence refers to an example with "” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” (like) is masked, thus in the char sequence, two tokens ”” _and_ ””" — which appears to be an incomplete OCR artifact. More importantly, while a loss function is provided for MVP_obj and MVP_pair, none is explicitly given for MVP_hier despite the template suggesting one should be. The prose only describes masking at the coarse-grained level, but does not specify how the loss is computed — is it only on the coarse-grained tokens via the aggregated representations? The absence of a formal loss function for MVP_hier, unlike the other two, creates an underspecification. Given that the model is evaluated, this is a notable omission.

comments.title: Ambiguity in aggregation method for MVP_hier and MVP_obj
comments.quote: For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the starting token's representation is used as the word's representation, which aligns with the BERT [CLS]-style pooling. However, in MVP_hier, it says that character embeddings are aggregated to form word representations before feeding into the transformer. This contradicts the later statement that the starting token's representation is used. If aggregation happens before the transformer, then pooling after would be redundant. It is ambiguous whether: (a) character embeddings are summed/mean-pooled before input, or (b) characters are input as a sequence and the first token's final-layer representation is used post-transformer. The latter is standard (as in BERT), but the former would be structurally different. This ambiguity affects model implementation and interpretation of how information flows.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to redesigning Chinese BERT vocabularies and multi-vocab pretraining strategies. The core ideas of combining CWS with subword tokenization (seg_tok) and the three MVP strategies are novel and potentially valuable contributions. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, covering multiple downstream tasks. However, the paper suffers from several technical issues including OCR-induced notation inconsistencies, missing mathematical details in loss functions, underspecified methodological choices, and some questionable claims without sufficient evidence. The most significant issues are the unclear vocabulary construction process for seg and the insufficient justification for key hyperparameters like λ.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for seg_tok
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, 'seg_tok' appears inconsistently as '_seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_' with formatting artifacts. While this appears to be an OCR issue where subscript formatting is lost, it creates confusion. The term appears both as 'seg tok' (with space) and '_seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_' in the same contexts. Given the context about combining segmentation and tokenization, the intended term is clearly 'seg_tok', but the inconsistent rendering could confuse readers about whether these refer to different concepts.

comments.title: Missing or corrupted loss function equations
comments.quote: The loss function for MVP _hier_ is [...] in which I _[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] _[ i]_[-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]
comments.explanation: The loss functions for all three MVP strategies appear to be corrupted or missing due to OCR errors. The text describes the loss functions but the mathematical equations are not properly rendered, with variables appearing as '[x] i_[is' and other artifacts. This makes it impossible to verify the mathematical formulations of the proposed methods, which is critical for understanding and reproducing the work.

comments.title: Unclear vocabulary construction for seg
comments.quote: b) count the words' frequency, and add the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ with frequency 0;
comments.explanation: Step b of the seg vocabulary construction is unclear and potentially contradictory. It says to 'add the tokens from seg_tok with frequency 0' but tokens with frequency 0 wouldn't exist in the corpus. This might mean 'add tokens from seg_tok regardless of their frequency in the corpus' but the phrasing is ambiguous. This step is crucial for understanding how the hybrid seg vocabulary is built.

comments.title: Underspecified aggregation method in MVP_obj
comments.quote: For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token's representation to represent the word's representation.[4] Denote **x** _[mask]_ and **y** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vf_ and _Vc_ respectively.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions using a 'BERT-style pooler' for aggregating character representations to word representations in MVP_obj, but doesn't fully specify this method. While footnote 4 acknowledges this limitation, the pooling strategy is critical for the auxiliary MLM task performance. More details about how exactly the aggregation works (e.g., whether it's just the first token or involves attention/weighting) would be needed for reproducibility.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about token sparsity
comments.quote: We believe that its disadvantages on sequence labeling tasks is due to sparcity of tokens, i.e., each token has less training samples, thus are less well trained for token level classification.
comments.explanation: The paper presents as a 'belief' rather than evidence the claim that seg_tok's worse performance on NER tasks is due to token sparsity. This is a significant claim that affects the interpretation of results, but no analysis is provided to support it (e.g., showing the frequency distribution of tokens, or ablation studies varying vocabulary size). The claim should either be supported with evidence or presented more cautiously.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in Table 1
comments.quote: MVP_pair_(_char, seg_|~~_t_~~_ok, s_)|87.04
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the notation for MVP_pair uses 's' (presumably for 'single') in some entries, but earlier in Section 3.2, the notation was specified as 'single' not 's'. This inconsistency could confuse readers about whether these represent different configurations. Given that 'e' (for ensemble) is used consistently, 's' should be clarified or made consistent with the earlier notation.

comments.title: Underspecified λ coefficient selection
comments.explanation: The paper tests λ values spanning two orders of magnitude (0.1 to 10.0) but provides no justification for this range or how it was chosen. The λ coefficient controls the relative importance of auxiliary objectives in MVP_obj and MVP_pair, which is crucial for model performance. Some rationale for the selected range or reference to prior work would strengthen the methodology.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper explores interesting methods for improving Chinese pre-trained language models through vocabulary redesign and multi-vocabulary training strategies. While the empirical results are compelling and the proposed approaches (seg_tok and MVP variants) are clearly motivated, the formal presentation suffers from notable gaps—specifically, the paper introduces loss functions for the MVP strategies (Section 3.2) but leaves the mathematical definitions incomplete or missing entirely within the provided text. Despite these formal gaps, the design and results are sufficiently transparent to follow the authors' core contributions.

comments.title: Missing loss function definitions
comments.quote:
The loss function for MVP _hier_ is


in which I _[x] i_[is a variable with binary values indicating whether the] _[ i]_[-th token is masked in] **[ x]**[.]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors introduce the loss function for MVP_hier but fail to provide the actual mathematical summation or formal definition. There is a blank space where the equation should be. While the surrounding text 'in which I[x]i is a variable...' suggests an intention to provide a formula, the lack of the formal expression makes the specific implementation details (e.g., how the hierarchical aggregation influences the loss) underspecified. This is a critical gap for reproducibility, as one must infer the loss structure solely from the textual description.

comments.title: Incomplete mathematical definition for MVP_obj
comments.quote:
Denote **x** _[mask]_ and **y** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vf_ and _Vc_ respectively. The loss function for MVP _obj_ is as follows:


in which I _[x] i_[and][I] _[y] i_[are][variables][with binary][values indicating whether][the] _[i]_[-th token][is masked][in] sequence x and y respectively.
comments.explanation: Similar to the issue with MVP_hier, the equation for the loss function of MVP_obj is missing. The text following the empty space describes the indicators, but without the actual equation, it is impossible to determine the exact weight or combination of the primary (fine-grained) and auxiliary (coarse-grained) MLM losses beyond the brief mention of the coefficient lambda.

comments.title: Missing formal definition for MVP_pair loss
comments.quote:
Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP _pair_ is


If after MVP _pair_ pretraining we decide to only keep one of the encoder, we will call this model single vocab MVP _pair_ .
comments.explanation: The section ends with a sentence fragment 'Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP pair is' followed by an empty line. The formal definition is completely absent. Given the complexity of the MVP_pair architecture involving parameter sharing and potential concatenation of losses from distinct encoders, the lack of a formal objective function is a significant omission.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated investigation into vocabulary design for Chinese PLMs and introduces several novel multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) strategies. The core idea of balancing character-level granularity with word-level semantics via segmentation and subword tokenization is sound and addresses a genuine gap in Chinese NLP. However, the manuscript contains a few critical presentation gaps due to OCR extraction (notably missing loss equations), a glaring typographical contradiction in the experimental analysis, and minor ambiguities in how masking alignment and auxiliary objectives are implemented across different granularities. With these corrections and clarifications, the methodology and empirical claims would be clear and reproducible.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel vocabulary design strategy for Chinese BERT, combining word segmentation with subword tokenization (denoted 'segtok'), along with three Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies. The experimental results demonstrate that 'segtok' improves efficiency and performance on sentence-level tasks, and MVP strategies further enhance sequence labeling capabilities. However, the submission presents significant readability issues. The core mathematical definitions of the loss functions are heavily corrupted by OCR artifacts, making the methodology difficult to verify. Additionally, the description of the MVP_pair ensemble finetuning method lacks necessary technical details regarding the aggregation and concatenation layers needed to reproduce the sequence labeling outputs.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes interesting vocabulary designs and multi-vocabulary pretraining strategies for Chinese BERT. However, it contains a critical inconsistency in the loss function for MVP_obj that contradicts the textual description. Additionally, the notation for MVP_pair is ambiguous regarding which vocab corresponds to which encoder and how the ensemble methods are applied. These issues need resolution to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Loss function for MVP_obj contradicts the textual description
comments.quote:
Denote x_[mask] and y_[mask] as the masked tokens under Vf and Vc respectively. The loss function for MVP_obj is as follows:
L = -∑_i I^y_i log p(y_i|...) - λ ∑_j I^x_j log p(x_j|...)
comments.explanation: The text states that the main MLM is on the fine-grained Vf and an auxiliary MLM is added on the coarse-grained Vc. However, in the loss function, the first term (without λ) is on y (coarse) and the second term (with λ) is on x (fine), reversing the roles. The definitions of x and y align x with Vf and y with Vc, so the loss should be L = -∑_i I^x_i log p(x_i) - λ ∑_j I^y_j log p(y_j). This is a major error that misrepresents the method and could mislead readers about the objective.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation and ordering in MVP_pair definitions
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j), where V1 and V2 are two different vocabs, i=s means only the encoder with V1 is kept for finetuning (single vocab model), and i=e means both encoders are kept (ensemble model).
comments.explanation: The notation suggests that V1 corresponds to the encoder kept in single mode, but in the results table, entries like MVP_pair(char, seg_tok, s) and MVP_pair(seg_tok, char, s) appear without clarifying which vocab is used for the left/right encoder. For ensemble, the meaning of ftc and ctf depends on which encoder is considered fine/coarse, and the ordering in the table (e.g., MVP_pair(seg_tok, char, e, ftc) vs MVP_pair(char, seg_tok, e, ctf)) is not clearly mapped to the descriptions. A reader cannot easily determine which combination of vocabs and ensemble strategy yields each reported result.

comments.title: Underspecified aggregation method for MVP_hier
comments.quote: representations of chars inside a word is aggregated into the representation of this word
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify how character representations are aggregated into a word representation for MVP_hier. While MVP_obj mentions using a BERT-style pooler (starting token), MVP_hier leaves the aggregation function unstated. This omission makes it difficult to reproduce the model precisely, as different aggregation choices (mean, max, attention, etc.) could affect performance.
